title: Part 17
text: foule’, or, as it would much later be known, the ‘jolly’ (the term is of nineteenth century American origin). Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 159 It had no value on its own, but it was almost always decisive: the one card that could turn around the whole game, and alter existing values, either by a nullifying or multiplying operation. In a technical sense the fool has no trick-making power: in most games (including tarot) it may be played at any time, irrespective of any obligation to follow suit or to play a trump. There can be little doubt that it was very much via card playing that the trickster figure diffused into popular culture in Europe. Strategically speaking, playing cards was of course very much about ‘tricking’ (and outright cheating – something almost impossible in chess and difficult with dice). Third, the practice of card playing introduced a completely new ‘social setting’ in a very concrete sense: four persons sitting around a square table. All early card games were played by four persons. European decks have four ‘types’ or ‘suits’, and the number of cards is always divisible by four (not three or five). This is a structural property of practically all the first card decks produced in Europe. The last thing to take notice of in this context is that very often two of the players were women. Judging from the available paintings, images and written records, card playing was particularly attractive for young women – and the playing of cards became connected to public flirting and sexual seduction. It was very much via card playing that women were drawn or ‘seduced’ into the public sphere. Fourth, card playing opened up whole new possibilities for gambling practices, ways of calculating and counting up wins and losses; and most of today’s gambling practices derive directly from cards. Venice and Institutionalized Gambling: The World’s First Casino It is therefore not surprising that in 1638 the first official, permanent casino was inaugurated in Venice, in the middle of the Thirty Years’ War. It went under the name, Il Ridotto. The Ridotto was important for two reasons: it was the world’s first government-sanctioned gambling house, and the first that was open to the general public. In short, the Ridotto makes Venice the birthplace of the casino. Il Ridotto is a wing of Venice’s San Moisè Palace, a nobleman’s villa and palace at the Campo San Moisè. 8 This was the first legal, public mercantile Casino ever. It was here that Casanova would often seduce women of the Venetian aristocracy. Placed in a palace named after Moses, 9 on a road close to the Canal Grande – in fact, just at the entrance of the Canal Grande – gambling became institutionalized. Casinos, 8 The building has now turned into a 3 star hotel. Visitors who stay there get free tickets to the casino. 9 Not a few ironies are at play here; the casino was also close to the Church of San Moisè (on Piazza San Marco), and next door to the Theatre of San Moisè. Moses is the figure of origin of Judaic and Christian morality and ethics, as we trace the Ten Commandments back to him. If we consider some of the commandments (thou shalt not steal; thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s house; thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that is thy neighbour’s) casino Liminality and the Modern 160 of course, must in and by themselves be seen as quintessentially liminal spaces, set apart from ordinary rules and norms for behaviour: an out-of-the-ordinary space where excitement thrives. The term ‘ridotto’ comes from the Italian word ‘riddure’, meaning to ‘close off’ or ‘make private’. It originally referred to several illegal, privately owned gambling clubs that offered games of chance to members of Venice’s nobility in the city’s Rialto district. The reference to the ‘private room’ is doubly fitting, as it also indicates the fact that prostitution flourished alongside the gambling activities (a persistent combination ever since). These clubs came into being after the Venetian authorities attempted to ban games of chance that had spontaneously sprung up in the city’s streets. Realizing that it could not effectively prevent citizens from wagering on dice and card games, the Great Council of Venice opened its Ridotto in 1638. Not surprisingly, they did so in a liminal moment: on the occasion of the city’s annual spring carnival. The world’s first casino was thus established to provide controlled gambling during the carnival season, but soon started to run for many months of the year – and this of course happened as the carnival developed into something like an almost permanent institution in Venice. Due to the high stakes and formal dress code, only nobles could afford to play at the casino’s tables; one such discriminating injunction, for instance, was that players had to wear three-cornered hats and masks in order to participate in Il Ridotto’s games. The casino was closed in the 1770s as the city government perceived it to impoverish the local gentry, that was gambling away its shrinking fortunes. In 1774 Venetian reformer Giorgio Pisani proposed to close the Ridotto ‘to preserve the piety, sound discipline and moderate behaviour’ of the city’s inhabitants. Pisani’s motion passed by an overwhelming majority and the casino closed its doors the same year. By then, however, the gambling genie had long since left the bottle; and the carnival had become a near-permanent state. This was not just a Venetian development: from the eighteenth century, professional gambling houses and lotteries started to spread across Europe. In a general vein, the carnivalesque, with its hybridization, mixing of codes, grotesque bodies and transgressions, remained a fascinating spectacle for eighteenth century writers. There was one Venetian citizen who played quite an important role in this development, and his story speaks in its own eccentric way to some of the central mechanisms at play: Giacomo Casanova. Casanova and the Spread of Gambling in Europe: A Trickster at Play Tracing Casanova’s real-life story is not a simple task. He obsessively avoided entanglements, never married, kept no permanent home and had no legally acknowledged children. He was always on the move. The only memorial of gambling broke each of them: casinos are precisely liminal spaces that mimetically instigate our desire to win and possess something which does not in principle belong to us. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 161 Casanova anywhere in the world is a stone plaque on a wall of the minuscule laneway Calle Malipiero in the San Samuele district, declaring that Casanova was born here in 1725 to two actors. His father, Gaetano, was also a dancer. At the moment of his birth, Calle Malipiero bore an even more fitting name: Calle della Commedia. Luckily, Casanova decided to write down his own story, one that is so full of incredible episodes that it leaves the reader in utter disbelief. But as time goes by, more and more details have actually been confirmed by historians. Casanova is one of the most intriguing figures in modern European history. He met and discussed with eighteenth century figures like Voltaire, Catherine the Great, Benjamin Franklin and probably Mozart. He made his living as a gambler, an astrologer and spy – or as anything his vivid imagination could come up with. He was a restless writer. He translated The Iliad into Venetian dialect, wrote a science fiction novel, a proto-feminist pamphlet, and a range of treatises on mathematics, chemistry and music. He was also one of history’s most incredible travellers, criss- crossing Europe from Madrid to Moscow, and from London to Constantinople. He would intrigue everybody: kings, princes, writers and statesmen – not to mention the thousands of women he seduced. He wrote his famous Story of My Life in his penniless old age, while working as a librarian at the Castle Dux, in the mountains of Bohemia. That the figure of Casanova in some pertinent way speaks to modern lifestyles has been noted by a series of writers, and among them, Georg Simmel. Simmel invokes Casanova with particular emphasis in his essay The Adventure, In this essay, Simmel tries to identify the nature of experience: what characterizes it as a psychological phenomenon? What are the attributes of adventure as a category of experience? Simmel’s entire discussion here also seems strongly influenced by Kierkegaard, both in the singling out of a person connected to an activity which comes to stand for a ‘position’ (as in ‘the stranger’), and in the substantial discussion of the nature of experience, very much linked to the temporal dimension. Casanova is the seducer by whom all other seducers in modernity are measured, the pure sensualist. He stands for Simmel as the Weberian ideal-type of the man who only lives to seek pleasure, as does don Giovanni for Kierkegaard in Either/Or. Within the continuous flow of events that make up the arc of this existence, Simmel writes, there are experiences that seem discontinuous from the rest: moments of intensity cut off from the sensory experiences of the everyday, islands that rise up from the daily events that wash over us and circulate around us. At the same time, Simmel argues that every single experience contains a modicum of the characteristics which, if they grow beyond a certain point, bring it to the ‘threshold’ of adventure. To Simmel, Casanova is the archetypical example of an adventurer, because he lives in ‘pure presentness’: For this reason, the adventurer is also the extreme example of the ahistorical individual, of the man who lives in the present. On the one hand, he is not determined by any past (and this marks the contrast between him and the aged, Liminality and the Modern 162 of which more later); nor, on the other hand, does the future exist for him. An extraordinary characteristic proof of this is that Casanova (as may be seen from his memoirs), in the course of his erotic-adventurous life, every so often seriously intended to marry a woman with whom he was in love at the time. In the light of his temperament and conduct of life, we can imagine nothing more obviously impossible, internally and externally. Casanova not only had excellent knowledge of men but also rare knowledge of himself. Although he must have said to himself that he could not stand marriage even two weeks and that the most miserable consequences of such a step would be quite unavoidable, his perspective on the future was wholly obliterated in the rapture of the moment. (Saying this, I mean to put the emphasis on the moment rather than on the rapture.) Because he was entirely dominated by the feeling of the present, he wanted to enter into a future relationship which was impossible precisely because his temperament was oriented to the present. (Simmel, the Adventurer, section 6) Casanova and Life as a Trickster Casanova (1725–1798) was not only born to actors, he had himself ‘theatre in his blood, and the world became his stage’ (Childs 1989: 1). The Venice of his time was completely given over to the pursuit of pleasure. By the early eighteenth century, the city was crowded with theatres and spontaneous street acts. There was gambling and gaming everywhere, and carnival, where everyone went masked and played identity games, extended over half the year (ibid.). In many ways, Casanova was born into this world of permanent liminality, but he also had a role to play in the way it spread outside his native city. It is well-known that Casanova was thrown in prison one July night in 1755, just after his 30th birthday, when police burst into his bedroom. In a society whose excesses were alternately indulged and controlled, he had been singled out by the Venetian Inquisition’s spies for prosecution as, amongst other things, a cheater, magician, freemason and a blasphemer (possibly the length of this list of accusations had something to do with his attentions to the mistress of one of the inquisitors). He was condemned for an undisclosed term in the prison cells known as The Leads, in the attic of the Doge’s Palace. There, Casanova languished for more than a year, until he made a daring and miraculous escape through the roof. His escape made Casanova a minor celebrity in the courts of Europe, but it also provoked his long exile from Venice, lasting eighteen years. Now his career as a traveling adventurer began in earnest. It has been estimated that he covered nearly 40,000 miles in his lifetime, mostly by stagecoach on bumpy eighteenth century roads – which is a lot. Throughout his life, he would sooner or later appear in every single European city that counted, out of nowhere, and somehow introduce himself at the inner circles of the powers that may be. He always left an impression. He was the ultimate self-invented man. After his famous escape, he ended up in Paris in January 1757 (Childs 1989: 77). Casanova was here able to exploit his connections to the French Freemasons Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 163 (Casanova had previously joined the Freemasons in Lyon in 1750). He immediately started to mingle with the aristocracy and continued to ‘play the chameleon’, as he himself liked to describe it. He looked for a patron, and found one in Francois Joachim de Pierre de Bernis, a French noble and later Cardinal. De Bernis had been French ambassador in Venice where he had come to know Casanova (they had shared mistresses and joined each other in rather frivolous ‘supper parties’) and was now a powerful minister, about to become Minister for Foreign Affairs. De Bernis presented Casanova to Pâris-Duverney, who in 1751 had been responsible for the establishment of the famous École Militaire. He was now seeking means of raising funds for this military institution. In general, France was in dire need of income to sustain its military efforts in war-prone Europe. It is in this context that Casanova came up with the brilliant idea of introducing a state-run lotto. Another Italian, Jean Calzabigi, was also present as the idea was presented to Pâris-Duverney; Calzabigi would himself later introduce state- run lotteries in many other parts of Europe, including Brussels and Berlin (here personally charged by Frederick the Great). In Paris the lottery was acclaimed by decree on August 15, 1757, a watershed date in European history. The lottery operation involved the drawing of five numbers from a total of 90, a system that has been reproduced in many European countries since. 10 The enterprise enjoyed immediate success, and the drawing of the lots intensified from as soon as 1758. Casanova became one of the trustees of the first state lottery, and also one of its best ticket salesmen. It made him rich very quickly and further contributed to his immense popularity in the Parisian salons. As someone who allegedly knew about numbers, Casanova also gained the reputation of being an alchemist. Casanova had studied chemistry from an early age and it was also in this quality that he became extremely popular in Enlightenment Paris. De Bernis recognized Casanova’s endless qualities and suggested employing Casanova as a spy for the French state, a role that he accepted. His first task was a successful mission to the French naval forces at Dunkirk, assembled for a descent on England. 11 During the Seven Years’ War, Casanova was again called upon to increase the state revenues for financing the war efforts, and was entrusted the mission to sell French state bonds in Amsterdam – which he did, again very 10 Lotteries were not a completely novel invention, nor within France. The first private lottery with monetary prices was apparently in Florence in 1530, and soon thereafter Venice created the world’s first government monopoly of lotteries (Brenner and Brenner 1990: 9). The novelty in eighteenth century France lay with the fact that lotteries now became the exclusive domain of the state (private lotteries became outlawed), and that the budgets involved grew immensely in size, playing with big numbers and scientific calculations of how to maximize gain; the connections to the rise of probability theory are particularly tight. In short, lotteries became an integral element of the modern reason of state. 11 When Casanova returned to Venice in 1774, he took up this trade again as he became a spy for the Venetian inquisitors of state – the very same institution that had condemned him to jail two decades earlier. Liminality and the Modern 164 successfully. He was even offered a job at the ministry of finance, which he duly refused (a good bluffer also knows his limits). It is difficult to find more direct connections between modern state formation, warfare, the social action of trickster figures and the evolution of the element of play in mass-consuming leisure culture. Another of the games that was in fact brought from Venice to France in this period was roulette, called Biribi in Venice; this became a very popular game of chance during the violent 1790s in Paris. The roulette, the little wheel, then became the most popular game of chance in German territories throughout the nineteenth century, and then in all of Europe, including Russia (Dostoyevsky not only wrote about the roulette, but also played it, passionately). Since then, gambling has consistently gained ever more space. State-run lotteries have remained precious vehicles for raising ‘voluntary’ funds from citizens. Casinos now generate incomes that make the other branches of entertainment industries pale into insignificance. The more gambling is prohibited, the more it apparently thrives. The US today tops the list of Casino revenues. It is this gambling industry which is spreading to every corner of our lives: to bars, super markets, Ryanair flights and to the limitless hyperspace of on-line virtual gambling; for while it was surely the internet and neo-liberal deregulation that jointly provoked the exponential growth of gambling that we have seen since the 1990s, the seeds of its growth were planted long before. Our Constructed Risk Societies In the form of liminality associated with gambling, logic functions side by side with passion; the rules of cause and effect cease to exist; luck, rather than hard work, reigns; and ordinary people are transformed in a magical moment into millionaires, champions – or homeless beggars. ‘Play mocks at work’, as Caillois says (2001: 45). The appeal is obvious: it shows us what could be, if only the world did not run by the normal rules. It is an invitation into another world, a world turned upside down. The craving for gambling, however, also speaks to something deeper concerning the implosion brought about by liminality in contemporary life. The hotness of liminal excitement and spectacle quickly turns into Baudrillard’s coldness of the simulacrum, where ‘Energies freeze in their own fire, they deter themselves’ (Baudrillard 1994: 40). In the hyper-reality captured by Baudrillard, every boundary between real and imaginary has been broken down. Implosion is eating up all the energies of the real, falling in upon itself, as everything becomes a simulation. The real is no longer, it can only be artificially produced. Seen from that perspective, permanent liminality is the ultimate destruction of the very possibility of meaning. The sociological relevance of the development we have sketched here is not confined to the gambling industry itself. It was Wall Street’s brand of ‘casino capitalism’ that crashed the American economy in 2008, paving the way for the global economic crisis we still live with. Not without reason, in Caillois’ discussion Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 165 of the ways in which the various types of games can be ‘corrupted’, the clear and obvious link between alea and the stock market economy is stressed. While lotteries and casinos are the evident examples of alea within what Caillois calls ‘cultural forms found at the margins of the social order’ (but as we have already seen, those margins are no longer marginal), speculation on stock markets is the single example listed of ‘institutional forms integrated into social life’ of alea (Caillois 2001: 54). One of the major themes in social theory during the last two decades is the notion that we live in a ‘risk society’, as argued most famously by Ulrich Beck. But was not life more risky and dangerous for our grandparents than for us? If today we live in a risk-society, and if this defines our historical epoch, it is perhaps first and foremost because we long for risks and actively seek them out: it is a self- imposed haunt, much more so than a social-empirical diagnosis of our objective living conditions. This development can be further illustrated by looking into another play phenomenon pertaining to the accelareting global entertainment industry: that of bungee jumping. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 7 From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid: Bungee Jumping and the Quest for Excitement in Contemporary Leisure ‘Today you can try your limit’ Free day in Queenstown, today you can try your limit! Options are: Bungee Jumping, Sky Diving, Gondola and Luge, Jet Boating, Parasailing, Paragliding, Canyon Swing – and much more !!! (Brochure description of Day 6 activities for a typical Adventure Tour in New Zealand, Christmas/New Year 2010/2011) On September 25, 2010, James Field made it into the record books, after completing a world record forty-two bungee jumps in one hour, repeatedly hurling himself from a platform fifty metres off the ground. Field, who smashed the existing record of nineteen jumps, said he had to concentrate hard as he leapt from the windswept ledge near Tower Bridge in central London. It was a case of focusing, because you’ve got a lot of blood rushing to your head ... It was a lot harder than I expected but you just have to get into the rhythm. I was having to get my breath back each time I went back up. Mary Oladapo, who was monitoring the Sheffield man’s effort for Guinness World Records, said: ‘He was looking a bit queasy by the end and I don’t blame him’. Despite the time constraints, safety was a priority. After each leap, the crane that Mr Field was attached to lowered him to the ground, where he was caught by his assistants. The ten metre bungee rope was then changed before the crane took him back up to the platform. This chapter is about bungee jumping and how it reflects the world in which we live. During the 1980s bungee jumping became one of the most popular ways of seeking a ‘real experience’, when the practice was commercialized and introduced as a leisure activity around the world. This happened in the same period as outdoor sport activities or ‘outdoor recreation’ exploded in kinds and numbers, clearly linked to the ‘experiential turn in tourism’ and the proliferation of ‘adventure tourism’. This development has only been reinforced since, with adventure tourism establishing itself as a widely popular category, together with a proliferating number of sub-disciplines, including rafting, snowcross, paragliding, Liminality and the Modern 168 skysurfing, cliff diving, paintballing, drifting and base jumping – to name but a few. The more dangerous of these are often termed ‘extreme sports’. A key feature of these activities is the experience of danger: going to the limits, or indeed, standing on the limit. Hence, bungee jumping can be seen as an apt metaphor for understanding liminality in contemporary tourism and leisure. We live in an ‘adventure’ culture, or a ‘kick culture’ (Boeve 1999). The original meaning of the term adventure still clings to it today, as in mediaeval French it referred to ‘chance’ or ‘luck’. In Latin (adventure) the word referred to something about to happen, a future event, about whose outcome one could not know, a future participle of advenire. The term therefore involved the idea of venire, which indicates a movement, but also an arrival. In the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, at the threshold of modernity, the word became tied to a dangerous experience, understood as a trial to go through. This ties the etymology to the conceptual discussion of experience, as a dangerous ‘passing through’. As has already been discussed, it was in the context of his comparative explorations of liminality that Victor Turner came to identify his own project with the philosophy of Dilthey (see for example Turner 1982: 12–19; 1988: 84–97; see also the discussion by Andrews 2009, who here links the conceptual debate explicitly to a discussion of contemporary leisure). Turner said that it takes an anthropologist to understand the significance of Dilthey (Turner 1985: 210). Via Dilthey, Turner sought to return to the perhaps greatest question of modern philosophy: how to understand experience. On the one hand, experience had been a cornerstone of both Cartesian and Kantian thought, hence of modern philosophy as such. In contrast to scholasticism and mediaeval philosophy, which were allegedly founded on ‘dogma’, post-Cartesian thought always claimed to derive from human experiences. On the other hand, and as discussed in Chapter 4, there was something deeply problematic about modern philosophy and its hold on experience – or rather, its non-hold. Within a Kantian framework, ‘experience’ was simply posited as ‘being there’, no longer open to investigation. To Kant, the world was chaotic, and order could only emerge through the imposing powers of the transcendental mind. This problematic attitude and starting point was exactly what Dilthey had realized with full clarity (on this point, see the important discussion by Ortega y Gasset 1965, 125–218). Dilthey introduced the term Erlebnis as an attempt to open up a new type of philosophical approach, departing from, and rooted in, ‘really lived experience’. The role of philosophy is not to impose order on the chaotic nature of empirical life, Dilthey said; the role of philosophy is to understand, from within and from the ‘middle’, the structure of experiences. In his late works, as we have also seen, Turner tentatively argued that Dilthey’s project could be sustained by an ethnographic platform, with reference to social drama performed by individuals who in the ritual act bring to the fore cultural essentials. Elaborating van Gennep’s work, Turner placed the triadic, sequential and processual structure of rites of passage at the core of anthropology. And he more than indicated that it was this structure that ‘forms’ experience, also at the From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid 169 philosophical level: that the order of experience is established from within the lived experience of passages. However, Turner had also suggested that in post-industrial societies such liminal rites would largely become replaced by the liminoid, i.e. by ‘out-of-the-ordinary’ experiences in leisure, arts and, indeed, sports. It is this discussion that we wish to engage in this chapter. Turner’s notion of the liminoid serves as a useful starting point for our discussion. However, at the same time it does not fully capture what is going on in contemporary leisure, characterized as it is by the almost panicked search for excitement and stimulation of the senses. These experiences involve no transformation of subjectivity and no passage to the ‘other world’; the bungee jumping industry can be taken to signify a further shift from the liminoid to what we here suggest to conceptualize as the ‘limivoid: the inciting of near-death experiences, a jump into nothingness, a desperate search for experience in a world of ontological excess. Searching for Extremes: Cross-cultural Perspectives Trivial as they may seem, practices of extreme sports and adventure tourism do point to something quite fundamental about what happens to liminality and to experience in contemporary culture. Many of these adventure activities involve near-death experiences. The continuous proliferation and development of these ‘adventure disciplines’ is in itself of interest. As frontline practitioners of an extreme sport have exhausted one type of extreme experience (which, evidently enough, after some time starts to feel less and less ‘extreme’) they consequently try to invent another one. When they succeed in establishing a minimum number of followers, the activity sometimes is ‘named’ and ‘branded’, and participants start to discuss and publicize their experience, often on-line. In some cases the activity is recognized as a discipline with national and international championships celebrated around the world. At this point, commercialization develops around the activity, also via television: sport or adventure channels transmitted globally dedicate an increasing amount of time to these ‘non- conventional’ sports. Besides the hugely popular Discovery Channel, Adventure, a whole series of specialized channels today only transmit extreme sports. The most popular one of these is probably The Extreme Sports Channel, a cable, satellite and internet TV channel that launched on May 1, 1999. It is broadcast twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, every day of the year into over sixty countries, is translated into twelve languages, and reaches millions of viewers every day. Together with the inclusion of these activities into tourist packages (often in softer forms than the ‘original’ to accommodate a broader paying audience), this ensures that extreme sports, although practised by relatively low numbers of persons at a regular level, has become part of a mass consumption. While a near-global phenomenon, the development is evidently driven by Western needs of consumption. Seemingly, most of adventure sports come from America, Liminality and the Modern 170 Australia and New Zealand, but with Europeans (or well-off non-Westerners) as equally frequent consumers. The price of a bungee jump normally lies somewhere between €100 and €200, depending on location and the ‘package’ of which the jump itself is a part. How are we to understand these developments? What is it about contemporary culture and mass consumption that is so in need of ‘adventure’? A key feature of these activities is the experience of danger and going to the limits. In fact, adventure tourism is often branded and sold with slogans such as ‘Try Your Limits’, or ‘Have a Real Experience’ (with all the due safety precautions written in small print, of
